尼古拉汽车及其创始人特雷弗·米尔顿(Trevor Milton)在一份研究报告揭露了多年的欺骗行为后，正争先恐后地挽回面子。在此之前，他们与通用汽车达成了新的协议，通用汽车似乎并不太担心他们的新合作伙伴过去的欺骗行为。
如果您一直关注我们对尼古拉汽车公司的报道，您知道我们对该公司的高度怀疑已经有一段时间了。
从针对特斯拉的专利欺诈诉讼到电池突破的可疑说法，该公司对我们几乎没有可信度。
2017年，特雷弗·米尔顿(Trevor Milton)邀请我测试他们的电动UTV，原型给我留下了深刻的印象，尽管动力总成主要是由博世制造的。
然而，在那次事件发生后，我对该公司的看法开始恶化，因为米尔顿决定向特斯拉提起20亿美元的诉讼，原因是有可疑指控称，特斯拉抄袭了特斯拉Semi的尼古拉One卡车设计。
看完整个诉讼后，我打电话给米尔顿，告诉他我认为整件事相当于专利欺诈，如果尼古拉开始从事专利欺诈活动，我不能把他们当回事。
尼古拉开始错过将其车辆投入生产的最后期限，但他们不断推出更多原型，并宣布与投资者和卡车运输公司达成协议，比如安海斯-布希(Anheuser-Busch)。
我所能确认的一位工程师正在为尼古拉的氢气/电动卡车的第一个原型工作，他说：
当特雷弗与投资者和政界人士一起参观会场时，听到他像水一样流淌出来的谎言，我感到震惊。
他声称弥尔顿会有源源不断的潜在合作伙伴和投资者进来，他会夸大卡车的能力，尼古拉所做的工作的程度，甚至他们拥有的技术与从货架上买来的技术相比。
今年早些时候，彭博社(Bloomberg)报道称，当米尔顿声称他在舞台上展示的尼古拉One是一款功能性卡车时，他撒了谎。
事实上，它是一个“推进器原型”，完全不能操作，尽管弥尔顿在舞台上特别声称它“不是一个推进器”。
在卡车上工作的同一名工程师告诉electrek，它在活动当晚甚至活动后几个月确实不起作用。
现在，兴登堡研究中心本周发布的一份新报告提供了更多关于弥尔顿和尼古拉欺骗行为的证据。
公平地说，希登堡持有尼古拉股票的空头头寸，但他们确实提供了一些清晰的证据，表明该公司存在一些欺骗性行为。
在报告中的十几项指控中，一些更具破坏性的指控包括一项指控，即尼古拉卡车实际驾驶的第一个视频是将卡车拖上山坡并将其推下的结果。
它得到了当时公司一名员工的短信支持，兴登堡找到了拍摄的实际地点。
另一项指控涉及一种模式，即声称他们开发和/或拥有一些实际上是刚刚从货架上购买的技术。
兴登堡举了米尔顿的例子，声称他们开发了自己的逆变器，并发布了一段视频，但他们发现这是一台卡斯卡迪亚逆变器，尼古拉只需在他们的标志上贴上掩膜胶带：
这一模式在宣布与通用汽车达成协议后特别有趣，根据协议，尼古拉将在尼古拉的獾电动皮卡和未来的车辆中使用该汽车制造商的电池和燃料电池技术。
尽管尼古拉多年来一直声称他们正在开发自己的电池组技术，但这仍然是事实。
米尔顿昨晚声称，他正在准备反驳欣登堡的说法：
我花了14个小时对兴登堡进行反驳，以便对这份报告提供一个清晰、事实和低调的回答。它将在市场开盘前推出，并通宵工作。我对每一个答案都感觉很棒。他们想要最大限度的损害，但没有奏效。现在回到增长中来。
他甚至补充说，只有胆小鬼才会跑去打架，但他刚才宣布不会再发表更多评论，而是决定请律师：
尼古拉聘请了外部律师Kirkland&amp；Ellis LLP，并授权他们直接联系SEC。这些指控是虚假的，具有欺骗性。然而，根据律师的建议，我现在不会进一步置评，只会说我们已经让SEC参与进来。现在掌握在他们手中，我就是pic.twitter.com/k1zh3Hymca。
当我们让通用汽车意识到他们刚刚通过与尼古拉合作而陷入的境地时，他们似乎并不太担心：
我们对携手合作将创造的价值充满信心。我们坚持我们在宣布这一关系时所作的声明。
作为他们成为尼古拉獾制造合作伙伴的交易的一部分，通用汽车收购了尼古拉汽车公司约11%的股份。自今天上午开盘以来，该股的价值已经缩水超过10亿美元。
就像我早些时候在推特上说的那样，这是尼古拉末日的开始：
我现在就叫它。尼古拉完蛋了。特雷弗暴露了。如果这份研究报告不是真的，他可以通过发布未经编辑的测试视频来毁掉它。他没有。相反，他请了律师。结束的开始。Https://t.co/hIGFDEMZJ5。
如果兴登堡的报道不准确，这些说法将很容易被揭穿。
他们只需发布未经编辑的视频版本，显示卡车靠自己的动力行驶，而不是滚落山坡。
如果他们没有这样做，很可能是因为他们做不到。
此外，对于报告中的其他一些说法，如卡斯卡迪亚逆变器，似乎没有任何否认。
这对我来说并不奇怪。但令人惊讶的是，他设法将通用汽车牵扯进来。来吧，通用汽车。你怎么会上当呢？
所有迹象都已经存在，他们有资源进行10倍于兴登堡在建立小型空头头寸之前的尽职调查。
在我看来，通用汽车在多次(EV1和博尔特EV之后的5年干旱)之后，太担心在电动汽车领域保持相关性，以至于他们看起来并没有太接近这笔交易。
我希望通用汽车的一些负责人能在这场惨败中翻滚，这将重新激励他们更认真地对待电动汽车。
note_en:
Nikola Motors and its founder, Trevor Milton, are scrambling to save face after a research report exposed years of deception. This follows their new deal with GM, who doesn’t seem very concerned about their new partners’ past deception.
  If you have been following our  coverage of Nikola Motors, you know that we have been highly skeptical of the company for some time
 From the patent trolling lawsuit against Tesla to dubious claims of battery breakthroughs, the company had very little to no credibility to us.
 In 2017, Trevor Milton invited me to test out their electric UTV, and I was impressed by the prototype, though the powertrain was mostly built by Bosch.
 However, my view of the company started to deteriorate after that event as Milton decided to hit Tesla with a $2 billion lawsuit over dubious allegations that they copied the Nikola One truck design for Tesla Semi.
 After reading the entire lawsuit, I called Milton and told him that I thought the whole thing amounted to patent trolling, and that I can’t take Nikola seriously if they start engaging in patent trolling activities.
  Nikola started missing deadlines to bring its vehicles to production, and yet they kept unveiling more prototypes and announcing deals with investors and trucking companies placing reservations, like Anheuser-Busch.
 An engineer who I was able to confirm worked on the first prototype of Nikola’s hydrogen/electric truck said:
 I was alarmed to hear lies flowing like water from Trevor as he toured the floor with investors and politicians.
 He claimed that Milton would have a constant stream of possible partners and investors come in, and he would exaggerate the capability of the truck, the extent of the work done by Nikola, and even what technology they owned versus what was bought off the shelves.
 Earlier this year,  Bloomberg reported that Milton lied when he claimed that the Nikola One he unveiled on stage was a functional truck.
  It was in fact a “pusher prototype” that was completely not operational, despite Milton specifically claiming on stage that it “wasn’t a pusher.”
 The same engineer who worked on the truck told  Electrek that it indeed wasn’t functional on the night of the event or even months after the event.
 Now a new report from  Hindenburg Research came out this week with even more evidence of Milton and Nikola’s deception.
 To be fair, Hidenburg has a short position on Nikola’s stock (NKLA), but they do bring some clear evidence of some deceptive behavior by the company.
 Among the dozen of claims in the report, some of the more damaging ones include an allegation that the first video of Nikola’s truck actually driving was the result of towing the truck up a hill and pushing it down.
 It is backed by text messages from an employee of the company at the time and Hindenburg finding the actual location of the filming.
 Another claims involves a pattern of claiming they delveoped and or own some piece of technology that was actually just bought off the shelves.
 Hindenburg gives the example of Milton claiming that they developed their own inverter and released a video of it, but they were able to figure out that it was a Cascadia inverter, and Nikola simply put masking tape over their logo:
  This pattern is especially interesting following the deal that was announced with GM, where Nikola would use the automaker’s battery and fuel cell technology in Nikola’s Badger electric pickup truck and future vehicles.
 That’s despite the fact that Nikola has been claiming for years that they are developping their own battery pack technology.
 Milton last night claimed that he was preparing a rebuttal of the claims made by Hindenburg:
 Been working on rebuttal of hindenburg for 14 hours to provide a clear, factual, low-emotional answer to the report. It will be out before market opens and working through the night. I feel great about every answer. They wanted max damage, it didn't work. Now back to growth.
 He even added that only cowards run for a fight, but he just now announced that he won’t be commenting further and instead decided to lawyer up:
 Nikola retained outside counsel Kirkland & Ellis LLP and authorized them to reach out directly to the SEC. The allegations are false and deceptive. On advice of counsel however, I won’t comment further now other than saying that we have involved SEC. It is in their hands and i  pic.twitter.com/k1Cz3Hymca
 When we made GM aware of the situation that they just got themselves into by partnering with Nikola, they didn’t seem too concerned:
 We are fully confident in the value we will create by working together. We stand by the statements we made in announcing the relationship.
 As part of their deal to be the manufacturing partner for the Nikola Badger, GM acquired around 11% of Nikola Motors. The stock has lost over $1B in value since opening this morning.
  Like I said earlier on Twitter, this is the beginning of the end for Nikola:
 Im calling it now. Nikola is done. Trevor is exposed. If the research report wasn't true, he could have destroyed it by simply releasing unedited video of the test. He didn't. Instead, he lawyers up. Begining of the end.  https://t.co/hIGFDEMZJ5
 If the Hindenburg report wasn’t accurate, the claims would be very easy to debunk.
  They simply have to release an unedited version of the video that shows the truck driving under its own power instead of rolling downhill.
 If they are not doing that, it is probably because they can’t do it.
 Also, as for some of the other claims in the report, like the Cascadia inverter, there doesn’t seem to be any denying of that.
  That’s not surprising to me. But what is surprising is that he managed to rope GM into this. Come on, GM. How could you fall for that?
 All the signs were there already, and they have the resources to do 10 times the due diligence that Hindenburg did before taking their small short position.
  The way I see it, GM is too concerned about staying relevant in the EV space after dropping the ball so many times (EV1 and 5-year dry spell after Bolt EV) that they didn’t look too close to that deal.
 I hope a few heads will roll at GM over this fiasco, and it will re-energize them to take EVs more seriously.
title: 尼古拉汽车被控大规模欺诈，谎言海洋
title_en: Nikola Motors Accused of Massive Fraud, Ocean of Lies

note:
在美国推出一年多后，MacRumors发现了一系列证据，表明Apple Card可能很快就会在国际上推出。
一位自称与一家参与支持澳大利亚‌‌Apple Card‌‌的匿名澳大利亚银行公司关系密切的消息人士告诉MacRumors，‌Apple Card‌最快将于今年年底在多个新地区推出。该提示猜测，澳大利亚发布的版本可能与iOS 14.1或iOS 14.2的发布时间一致，但警告称，可能要到2021年初iOS 14.3发布时才能准备就绪。
此外，苹果最近发布广告招聘几个新的苹果卡产品经理职位，这些职位涉及旅行以及与支付网络、银行发行商和其他参与者等外部合作伙伴的合作。在美国，高盛是‌苹果卡‌&#34；唯一的银行发行商。这份工作描述可能表明，苹果正在与美国境内的其他发行商进行谈判，或者更有可能的是，与全球其他地区的发行商进行谈判，以支持‌Apple Card‌。
MacRumors还发现在iOS14Beta8代码中引用了‌Apple Card‌的GDPR。一般数据保护条例(GDPR)是欧盟关于数据保护和隐私的法律规定。由于‌Apple Card‌目前还不能在美国以外的地区使用，因此似乎很奇怪，‌Apple Card‌的代码可能涉及欧洲的隐私权立法。
在属性列表中找到的代码似乎也存在于某些早期版本的iOS中。目前还不清楚该法规是专门指的是一般数据保护条例，还是其他什么的缩写。
在周二的苹果发布会上，苹果可能准备很快宣布‌Apple Card‌的国际扩张。或者，苹果可能会在传言中的10月份的活动上宣布与新款iPhone一起在国际上发布。
考虑到世界各地信贷市场和监管的差异程度，苹果要在多个新地区同时发布‌Apple Card‌将是一个艰巨的任务。然而，随着‌Apple Card‌在美国的成功，苹果进军国际市场将是有意义的。苹果可能会在周二宣布广泛的国际扩张，然后在其他地区逐步完成发布，速度要慢得多，因为它们已经准备好了。
在去年访问德国时，苹果首席执行官蒂姆·库克证实了他有兴趣在国际上推出‌Apple Card‌。上个月，三星在英国推出了三星支付卡(Samsung Pay Card)，以期在苹果之前在该地区站稳脚跟。

Over a year after its  U.S. launch,  MacRumors has uncovered a range of evidence to suggest that  Apple Card may soon be launching internationally.
  A source claiming to be close to an unnamed Australian banking corporation involved in supporting ‌‌Apple Card‌‌ in Australia tells  MacRumors that ‌Apple Card‌ will be launching in multiple new regions as soon as the end of this year. The tip speculated that the Australian release may coincide with the release of iOS 14.1 or iOS 14.2, but cautioned that it might not be ready until an iOS 14.3 release in early 2021.
 In addition, Apple has recently advertised for several new  Apple Card Product Manager roles that involve travel and "working with external partners such as payment networks, bank issuers and other players." In the U.S., Goldman Sachs is ‌Apple Card‌'s only bank issuer. The job description may suggest that Apple is setting about negotiation with other issuers within the U.S., or more likely, issuers to support ‌Apple Card‌ in other regions around the world.
 MacRumors has also discovered references to GDPR for ‌Apple Card‌ in  iOS 14 Beta 8 code. General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) is a regulation in EU law on data protection and privacy. As ‌Apple Card‌ is not currently available outside of the U.S., it seems curious that there is code potentially referring to European privacy legislation for ‌Apple Card‌.
 The code, found in a property list, also seems to have been present in some previous versions of iOS. It is unclear if the code is specifically referring to General Data Protection Regulation, or is an abbreviation for something else.
 With an  Apple event on Tuesday, Apple may be preparing to announce an international expansion of ‌Apple Card‌ imminently. Alternately, Apple could announce the international launch alongside new iPhones at a  rumored October event.
 Considering the extent to which credit markets and regulation varies around the world, it would be a tall order for Apple to release ‌Apple Card‌ in multiple new regions simultaneously. However, following the success of ‌Apple Card‌ in the U.S., it would make sense for Apple to expand into international markets. It is possible that Apple could announce a broad international expansion on Tuesday, before gradually completing launches at a much slower pace in other regions as they become ready.
 While visiting Germany last year, Apple CEO  Tim Cook  confirmed his interest in launching ‌Apple Card‌ internationally. Last month, Samsung  launched Samsung Pay Card in the UK, in an effort to establish a foothold in the region before Apple does.
 Sounds interesting. I haven't paid much attention to how it works and it may very well be different from the US in other territories, however if they launch in the UK I'll be checking it out. I always pay cards off and never keep any outstanding balance, however if they do some kind of cash back or other incentives then it might be a useful addition to the [virtual] wallet.

每样东西都有名字：孩子、宠物、汽车和地方。这是我们与主体和客体成为朋友的方式，也是我们回忆标识符的方式。但是，给一个品牌命名可能是一个痛苦的过程。这可能需要很长时间，有时，它似乎永远不会真正走到一起。为了减轻寻找完美名字的压力，记住企业创造品牌是有帮助的，而不是反之亦然。想想Mountain Dew(听起来很麻烦)，Starbucks(听起来不相干)和Virgin(听起来像是缺乏经验)，The Bull Company(听起来是负面的)，Slake(听起来像是它所做的事情的反面)。归因的意义和感觉是在命名后的几年里建立起来的。
首先也是最重要的，名字不是单独出现的。将有上下文元素，如标语、徽标、调色板，用于各种组件，如网站的顶部视觉，或广告的标题。所有这些都可以充斥着反映公司战略的文字和视觉效果。然而，名称是所有品牌组成部分中最核心、历史最悠久的。
向专业命名人员寻求帮助，或者自己找一个名字。以下基石构成了一个流程，并提供了一份良好的清单。
你的战略中最重要的方面，你的产品(是什么)，它代表什么(定位)，它代表谁(目标市场)，需要在最重要的品牌模块中清楚地表达出来：名称，标志和口号。
公司或产品越不为人所知，在品牌传播中需要展示的这些方面就越直接。
元描述：从突发新闻和娱乐到体育和政治，用所有的现场评论获取完整的故事。(目标市场：面向寻求多种意见的新闻娱乐读者)。
视觉效果和其他副本：员工照片位于前台。与描述价值观的文案并驾齐驱：“不仅是著名的咖啡，还有联系。我们是邻居聚会的地方，是你们日常生活的一部分。了解我们，你就会发现：我们远不止我们酿造的那样。我们称我们的员工为合作伙伴，因为我们都是共享成功的合作伙伴。我们确保我们做的每一件事都是通过人性的视角--从我们对世界上最高质量咖啡的承诺，到我们与客户和社区进行负责任的业务往来的方式。“。(目标市场：面向寻求社区的人群)。
在命名过程开始时，查找可用的域可能会有所帮助。这也将有助于寻找竞争对手。如果域名不可用，可以预先或附加一个额外的行业标识符，如书店的图书或任何与食品相关的品牌的食品或食品。想想ZolaBooks.com吧。
由于缺乏意义，应该避免使用像“解决方案”这样的宽泛术语(每个人都可以使用的词语不会增加意义)。公司的.com或非营利性的dot-org域名仍然最强大，在美国是首选。
通常情况下，好的名字会更短，否则它们会被自然地缩写成首字母缩写。想想肯德基、A.T.&amp；T和P&amp；G。一个名字有时由两三个复合词组成--这些词本身和它们的第一个字母需要很好的组合。党卫军在一些国家总是有不好的内涵。不管人们多么努力地禁止使用字母组合，最终，这些字母组合都会变成文件名，变成图标或其他折叠的徽标版本，变成代码，特别是当产品是自然出现截断版本的技术产品时。
B2C品牌专注于展示个性、文化和定位的名称。常见的名字有苹果(容易获得)、耐克和奥迪。品牌名称不一定要与实际产品联系起来，尽管它们当然可以联系起来(想想Netflix和伊索)，但可以添加一个附录，当一个品牌知名时，它可能会被删除。想想星巴克咖啡公司对阵星巴克。短/有趣/酷/平易近人/难忘/指示性胜过逻辑性和长期性。想想Hulu、柯达、Etsy、玛氏、3M、Nest和索尼。
避免公司名称使用描述性类别或常见的行业特定关键字名称。竞争对手会选择类似的品牌，这不仅会让你的品牌融入其中，还会浪费给公司定位的机会。阅读“纽约时报”关于EasyMail、MegaMail和ProMail的报道。此外，描述性名称更难注册商标，而且可能无法通过谷歌搜索找到。
创始人的名字被认为是功能性名称。想想戴尔、福特和高露洁。从一开始就具有个性和定位并不是必然的。
以B到B为重点的品牌通常有一个更具描述性的名称；它们会被缩写，这没什么。想想UPS、IBM、AWS。对于B到B的名字来说，有一件事很重要，那就是它们需要有潜力听起来很大，或者至少不是小的，而且绝对不会限制公司销售的服务。Bluecore的名字是Triggermail。触发电子邮件是该公司最初的做法，但后来又增加了其他产品。
更长的名字，一组名字和化合物，听起来自然有变小的趋势，除非它们包括(读作：拥有)该行业的一个重要分支或位置。想想全食超市、自然历史博物馆、美国航空公司。
一个名字应该广泛地适用于它的同龄人的领域(阅读：以正确的方式脱颖而出)。它应该很容易记住，没有复杂的拼写(或经过深思熟虑或用户测试的替代拼写)，只能以一种方式发音*而且它应该在行业内拥有。域名检查和商标数据库搜索将会有所帮助，但咨询商标律师，审查特定类别、州、国内和国际使用的使用情况，是一笔划算的资金。
*前汽车品牌大宇(Daewoo)在1995年将其难以发音的名字变成了一场广告宣传活动。发格酸奶还需要在包装上印上发音。在国际测试之前创建的其他名称包括华为、雷诺、豪雅和Moschino。普林斯顿大学(Princeton University)的两位心理学家在2006年的一项研究中发现，“人们更有可能购买新发行的、名字容易发音的股票。”
不同的时代，不同的约束，不同的趋势。对于互联网公司来说，-ster(Napster，Friendster)始于2001年，“my”发生在2005年(Myspace)，-fy/-ly/-y出现在2010年(Bitly，Artsy和Weeble)。为了更容易地注册商标和获得域名而放弃元音的做法早在2000年中期就开始了(Flickr、Tumblr、Unbxd、Scribd)。时尚界最常见的随机情侣三明治名字在2010年激增。想想Rag&amp；bone，Kit and Ace，Boll&amp；Branch。
一般来说，产品的命名(与公司的命名相反)可能会更时髦，而不是更时髦，因为它们随着市场的发展而演变，或者随着时间的推移，它们有改造的空间。
至于许多东西，从长远来看，高雅才是赢家，因为它永远不会过时。
缩写的公司名称应全部大写，而不是小写。常规名称应该拼写在一起，第一个字母大写。名称应用空格分隔，如果名称由多个单词组成，则应用连字符分隔，每个第一个字母都要大写。当复合词拼写在一起时，复合词的发音只能有一种方式，如果没有，就需要分开发音。一个很好的拼写测试是将名字插入长短形式的副本中：在句子的中间、句子的开头、项目符号列表和签名中。名称需要在所有应用程序中具有一致的外观，并且不会看起来很笨拙(考虑在句子开头使用全小写的品牌名称)。
选项一：徽标的小写拼写(比如亚马逊(Amazon)、阿迪达斯(Adidas)、花旗银行(Citibank)和英特尔(Intel)、邮报(MailChimp)、西榆树(West Elm)、易趣(EBay))会让公司变得更平易近人、更随意、更年轻，或者显示出对科技的关注。然而，在文本(普通文本或标题)中，品牌名称仍应以大写字母开头(在亚马逊上销售；2016年，邮猩猩宣布…)。。
选项2“永恒”：以大写字母开头，如果一个以上的单词是一个已经成长起来并拥有其身份的品牌，则用空格或连字符分隔：Squaepace、可口可乐、微软、The Container Store、沃尔玛(Walmart)。
选项3：所有的大写字母都是另一种永恒的用途，通常是为了让字体看起来更统一或更坚固而做出的设计决定。想想宜家(在文本中也拼写为Call Caps)，MUJI(文本中拼写为Muji)，Zara(文本中为Zara)，Costco(文本中为Costco)，家得宝(文本中为The Home Depot)，Netflix(文本中为Netflix)。
选项4：大写字母开头，但复合词之间没有空格，强调两者的词根，以显示团结和力量。可能在排版上不那么吸引人，也更难记住如何正确拼写：联邦快递(FedEx)、麦当劳(McDonalds)、红牛(RedBull)、HomeGoods、YouTube。
不是最重要的，但起始字母是另一个需要考虑的方面。你的品牌将按字母顺序列在某个地方：作为市场中的一种技术产品，在一系列合作伙伴中，在印刷目录中-品牌出现得越高，人们就会自然地接触到你的品牌，因为人们更频繁地看到第一部分。
贝佐斯将他的在线书店Cadabra更名为亚马逊，因为当时网站列表是按字母顺序排列的。最重要的是，据说在早期，人们在电话中听到卡达布拉的声音时，就会指出它听起来像身体。快点说这个名字，慢点说，连续说十遍，看看它是否容易脱口而出。口碑是最好的营销方式，人们不会说不会发音的话。
首先，测试其他语言的声音和意义，如果另一个市场可以相关的话。其次，把名字或三个版本从你的直接目标市场发送给10个人。询问他们认为这是一种什么样的产品，这个名字是否听起来很好听，是否会有负面的联想。没有必要挑选每个人都喜欢的东西，这只是为了检查无法弥补的疏忽，权衡利弊。
开始命名的一个很好的资源是Eli Altman的练习册“不要这样叫它”。
note_en:
Everything has a name: a child, a pet, cars and places. It’s how we befriend subjects and objects and it’s how we recall identifiers. But, naming a brand can be a painful process. It can take a long time and sometimes, it seems to never really come together. In order to take pressure off of finding the perfect name it’s helpful to remember that businesses create brands, not vice versa. Think  Mountain Dew (sounds cumbersome),  Starbucks (sounds unrelated) and  Virgin (sounds like a lack of experience),  The Boring Company (sounds negative),  Slack (sounds like the opposite of what it does). Attributed meaning and feelings build in the years after naming.
    First and foremost, a name doesn’t come alone. There will be contextual elements like a tagline, a logo, a color palette that are used on a variety of components, like the top visual on a website, or a headline on an ad. All of those can be filled with words and visuals that reflect a company’s strategy. A name, however, is a central and the longest-standing of all brand components.
 Get help from a naming professional or find a name yourself. The following cornerstones make for a process and provide for a good checklist.
 The most important aspects of your strategy,  your product ( what it is),  what does it stand for ( positioning),  who’s it for ( target market), need to get across clearly in the most important brand modules: the name, the logo and a tagline.
 The more unknown the company or product, the more direct those aspects need to be laid out in branding communications.
  Meta description: From breaking news and entertainment to sports and politics, get the full story with all the live commentary. (target market: for news and entertainment readers that look for a variety of opinions)
 Visuals and additional copy: Employee photos are in the foreground. Alongside copy that describes values: “not only celebrated coffee but also connection. We’re a neighborhood gathering place, a part of your daily routine. Get to know us and you’ll see: we are so much more than what we brew. We call our employees partners because we are all partners in shared success. We make sure everything we do is through the lens of humanity—from our commitment to the highest-quality coffee in the world, to the way we engage with our customers and communities to do business responsibly.” (target market: for people that seek community)
  At the beginning of the naming process, looking up domains that are available can be helpful. It’ll help find competitors as well. If the domain name is not available, an additional industry identifier can be pre or appended, like –books for a bookstore or –foods or eat– for any food-related brands. Think  zolabooks.com.
 Broad terms like  Solutions should be avoided due to lack of meaning (words that can be used by everybody don’t add meaning). Dot-com for companies or dot-org domains for nonprofits remain strongest and are preferred in the U.S.
  Usually, good names are shorter, otherwise they’ll get abbreviated naturally later into an acronym. Think  KFC, A.T. & T. and P&G. A name sometimes consists of two or three compound words—the words themselves and their first letters need to make a good combination.  SS will always have a bad connotation in some countries. No matter how hard one tries to prohibit the usage of monograms, eventually, these letter combos will make their way into file names, into favicons or other collapsed logo versions, into code, especially used when the product is a technical product where truncated versions occur naturally.
 B2C brands focus on names that show personality, culture and positioning. Common names are  Apple (easy and obtainable),  Nike and Audi. Brand names do not have to be connected to the actual product, although they certainly can be (think  Netflix and Aesop), but an appendix can be added that has the potential to be dropped when a brand is well-known. Think  Starbucks Coffee Company vs. Starbucks. Short/ fun/ cool/ approachable/ memorable/ indicative trumps logical and longer. Think  Hulu, Kodak, Etsy, Mars, 3M, Nest and Sony.
 Avoid a descriptive category or common industry-specific keyword name for a company name. Competitors will pick similar ones which will not only make your brand blend in but the chance to position the company is wasted. Read the  NYT story on EasyMail, MegaMail, and ProMail. Also, descriptive names are harder to trademark and can be impossible to find through Google search.
 Founders’ names are considered functional names. Think  Dell, Ford and Colgate. Carrying personality and positioning from the start is not a given.
 B to B-focused brands often have a more descriptive name; they will get abbreviated and that’s okay. Think  UPS, IBM, AWS. The one thing that’s important for B to B names is that they need to have the potential to sound big, or at least not small and definitely not limiting in what services the company sells.  Bluecore’s first name was TriggerMail. Triggering an email was how the company started out but other products were added on later.
  Longer names, a group of names and compounds naturally have a tendency to sound smaller unless they include (read:  own) an important branch or location of the industry. Think  Whole Foods, Natural History Museum, American Airlines.
 A name should fit broadly into the field of its peers (read:  stand out the right way). It should be easy to remember, have no complicated spelling (or a well-considered or user-tested alternative spelling), be pronounceable in one way only* and it should be ownable within its industry. A domain check and a search through the  trademark database will be of help, but consulting a trademark lawyer who reviews usage in specific categories, states, national and international use, is money well-invested.
 * The former car brand Daewoo turned their unpronounceable name into an ad campaign in 1995. Fage yoghurt still needs to have its pronunciation printed on its packaging. Other names that were created before being tested internationally are Huawei, Renault, Tag Heuer and Moschino. Two Princeton University psychologists have found in a   2006 study  that “people are more likely to purchase newly offered stocks that have easily pronounced names.”
  Different times, different constraints, different trends. For internet companies, the  -ster  (Napster, Friendster) started in 2001,  “My” happened in 2005 (Myspace), and  -fy/ -ly/ -y spiked in 2010  (Bitly, Artsy and Weebly). Vowel-dropping for easier trademarking and domain availability was early to mid 2000’s  (Flickr, Tumblr, Unbxd, Scribd). The random couple sandwich names mostly found in the fashion industry spiked in 2010. Think  Rag & Bone, Kit and Ace, Boll & Branch.
 Generally, the naming of products (as opposed to the naming of companies) can be more trendy versus classy because they evolve with the market or they have room for a revamp over time.
  As for many things,  classy wins in the long run because it never goes out of style.
  An abbreviated company name should be spelled in all caps, not lowercase. A regular name should be spelled together, first letter capitalized. A name should be separated by space or by hyphen if it consists of multiple words, capitalizing each first letter. When compound words are spelled together, there must be only one way to pronounce the compound, if not, it needs separation. A good test for spelling is to insert the name into long- and short-form copy: in the middle of a sentence, at the beginning of a sentence, into a bulleted list and into a signature. The name needs to have a consistent appearance across all applications and not look awkward (think an all lowercase brand name at the beginning of a sentence).
 Option 1 “Friendly”: lowercase spelling in logos (think amazon, adidas, citibank and intel, mailchimp, west elm, ebay) makes companies more approachable, casual, younger or shows a focus on tech. However, within text (general copy or headlines), brand names should still start with a capital letter (Sell on Amazon; In 2016, Mailchimp announced …).
 Option 2 “Timeless”: Leading capital letters, separated by space or hyphen if more than one word is a brand that has grown up and owns its identity: Squarespace, Coca-Cola, Microsoft, The Container Store, Walmart.
 Option 3: All caps is another timeless use and often a design decision to give the type a more unified or stronger look. Think IKEA (also spelled in call caps within text), MUJI (spelled Muji within text), ZARA (in text: Zara), COSTCO (in text: Costco), THE HOME DEPOT (in text: The Home Depot), NETFLIX (in text: Netflix).
 Option 4: Leading capital letters but no spaces between compounds shows unity and strength by keeping an emphasis on the roots of both. Can be typographically less attractive and are harder to remember how to spell correctly: FedEx, McDonalds, RedBull, HomeGoods, YouTube.
  Not the most important thing but the starting letter is another aspect to consider. Your brand will be listed somewhere alphabetically: as a tech product within a marketplace, in a logo series of partnerships, in a print catalog—the higher the brand shows up the more people will engage with your brand naturally because people see the first section more often.
 Bezos renamed his online bookstore  Cadabra to  Amazon because back then website listings were in alphabetical order. On top, it is said that in those early days people pointed out that  Cadabra sounds like  cadaver when heard over the phone. Say the name fast, say it slow, say it ten times in a row and see if it rolls off the tongue easily. Word of mouth is the best marketing and people don’t say things they can’t pronounce.
  First, test sound and meaning in other languages if another market can be relevant. Second, send the name or three versions of it to ten people from your direct target market. Ask what kind of product they think this is, if the name has a good ring to it and if there are negative associations coming up. There’s no need to pick something that everybody likes, this is simply for checking for irreparable oversights and weighing upsides against downsides.
 A good resource to get started with naming is a workbook by Eli Altman “ Don’t call it that.”
title: 品牌命名的原则

NBC的钟声本身就是无线电历史的声音。也许没有哪一种声音能比这更能让人回想起无线电的黄金时代。NBC的钟声-音符G-E-C-在NBC广播节目结束时播放，从NBC网络成立后不久开始，并在NBC广播和电视上继续日常使用，直到1971年。
1926年，在国家广播公司成立后不久，网络主管们意识到，附属电视台对于节目的确切结束时间，以及什么时候可以安全地切断地方广播，感到困惑。这个问题被指派给一个由三人组成的委员会：NBC工程总监、前AT&amp；T工程师奥斯卡·B·汉森(Oscar B.Hanson)、NBC管弦乐队指挥欧内斯特·拉·普拉达(Earnest La Prada)和NBC播音员菲利普斯·卡林(Phillips Carlin)。他们认为，某种有趣的信号将是表明所有节目结束的合适方式。当时，广播电台普遍使用钟声、锣声、警报声和其他机械设备的声音作为电台的标志性声音，因此NBC选择钟声并不罕见，也不是什么特别的创新。事实上，有一些证据表明，编钟的灵感可能来自当时美国全国广播公司(NBC)亚特兰大分部WSB使用的类似编钟序列。
在1927年至1928年期间，该委员会试验了几种纸币的组合。最初使用的七个音符序列，G-C-F-E-G-C-E，被确定为过于复杂，以至于播音员无法在一致的基础上正确演奏。它首先被简化为G-C-F-E，最后被简化为G-E-C。1929年11月29日，人们第一次听到了这一熟悉的序列。
钟声在每小时的29：30和59：30响起，表示当地电视台30秒休息的开始。他们最初是由播音员用一套手持铃声高举到麦克风上击打的。但是，编钟在节奏、音量和精确的计时上都有不一致之处。最后，人们确定解决这些问题的最好方法是机械地生成编钟。
设计钟声机器的人是理查德·H·兰格船长，他也是电子琴和RCA传真机的发明者。游骑兵创造了一个类似音乐盒的装置，手指在旋转的鼓上拨动一组芦苇。有三套八片簧片，每个音符一片，这样就可以产生基本音符加上几个泛音。每个簧片在振荡器电路中形成一片电容，所有簧片产生的信号由一根6C6五极管放大。它是由一个计时器激活的，计时器会在节目结束前两秒切断它(无论它是否结束！)。并将钟声提供给网络。NBC制造了数量有限的编钟机。旧金山的美国全国广播公司(NBC)有两台机器-主要的和备份的机器。另一些安装在全国各地网络程序发源地的其他城市-洛杉矶、芝加哥、纽约，可能还有其他几个城市。很可能曾经制造过的编钟机不超过12台。
这一页上的照片显示了为数不多的仍然存在的编钟机器之一，现在掌握在一位私人收藏家手中。(NBC在其整个历史上都有丢弃大量历史文物的短视习惯。正是由于几位NBC员工的远见卓识，他们从垃圾桶里救出了一些这样的物品，我们今天才能体验到那个时代的许多录制的节目、照片和其他纪念品。)。所显示的单元是序号为2的编钟机，可能来自第一组制造的编钟机。它的机械部件虽然做工精细，但似乎是手工制作的。这台机器无疑是美国全国广播公司在旧金山萨特街111号的演播室里使用的原始编钟机器。示意图还显示，序列号5是1933年制造的，因此这台机器应该早于它。主柜包含电机驱动簧片机构和放大器，可通过卸下前面板的四个指旋螺钉进入。该装置由外部电源操作，无疑与演播室中操作音频放大器的电池和电机发电机系统相同。较小的盒子包含定时器和开关，用于操作演播室和Nemo&34；广播线的钟声。尼莫(&#34；Nemo&34；)是早期无线电中用来表示远程广播的一个术语。它来自一个电话术语，代表不发散的总部。)。钟表机可以由时钟以自动模式操作，这是通常的操作方法，或者在诸如体育广播等具有不精确结束时间的节目的情况下由播音员手动操作。
*NBC钟声于1950年正式在美国专利局注册为注册服务商标，这是已知的第一起接受声音商标的案例
我刚刚看到你在旧金山湾区电台博物馆的页面上专门介绍了NBC的钟声机器，我想给你补充另一个历史事实。1969年，当我去美国全国广播公司(NBC)旗下的WKYC克利夫兰担任夏季更换工程师(董事会OP)时，货架上还放着一台编钟机。由于所有的O&amp；O&A&O&39；都起源于Noth(New On The Hour)和监视器插件等程序，所以它们都有两台钟机。除了WNBC/纽约，WMAQ/芝加哥和KNBR/旧金山，WKYC/克利夫兰和WRC/华盛顿也有编钟机。
到了第二年夏天，我在那里工作时，钟声机器不见了--我说不准是扔掉了，还是被某个老前辈带回了家。
最后一点无线电琐事：1987年8月，我正在为NBC广播网做附属公司关系工作，在Nabet工程师罢工期间，我们这些有技术背景的管理层轮流管理Noth的董事会，并在Radio Central(网络总控)配备Rod(无线电操作台)。当工程师们在那天早上6点离开时，我是董事会中的第一个管理人员，负责设计6、7、8和9点的Noth(这位天才是加里·纳恩，现在CBS广播网已经听到了他的声音)。那天早上8点30分，总经理克雷格·西蒙告诉我，电视网已经卖给了韦斯特伍德一号，钟声不是出售的一部分，所以我被禁止像往常一样在新闻节目结束时播放它们。
我不理他，像往常一样在上午9点的新闻节目结束时插话。几分钟后，他带着滑稽的笑容来到演播室，告诉我要确保没有其他人这样做。我把钟声推车和后备带出了演播室，确保了这一点。我建立了备份，并将最后一辆带有NBC编钟的购物车(直接从WNBC编钟机器录制)放入我的收藏中。
“参考文献：全国广播公司钟声的历史”(A History Of The NBC Chimes)，比尔·哈里斯·莫尔(Bill Harris More)的“全国广播公司钟声”(The NBC Chimes)，布莱恩·韦翰(Brian Wickham)的“无线电城的后台之旅”(Back后台造访无线电城)，作者弗雷德·克罗克(Fred Krock)
note_en:
The sound of the NBC chimes is the sound of radio history itself. Probably no single sound better recalls the golden age of radio. The NBC chimes – the musical notes G-E-C – were played at the end of every NBC radio program beginning shortly after the network's inception, and continued in daily use on NBC radio and television until 1971.
 Shortly after the formation of the National Broadcasting Companyin 1926, network executives became aware of confusion among theaffiliate stations as to the exact when a program ended, and when itwas safe to cut away for local announcements. The problem was assignedto a committee of three: Oscar B. Hanson, NBC's Director of Engineerand a former AT&T engineer; Earnest la Prada, an NBC orchestraleader; and Phillips Carlin, an NBC announcer. They decided that amusical signal of some kind would be an appropriate way to indicate theending of all programs. At that time, it was common for radio stationsto use the sounds of chimes, gongs, sirens and other mechanical devicesas a signature sound for their station, so the choice of a chime by NBCwas not unusual or particularly innovative. There is in fact someevidence that the chimes may have been inspired by a similar chimesequence used at that time by NBC affiliate WSB in Atlanta.
 During 1927 and 1928, the committee experimented with several combinations of notes. A seven-note sequence which was first used, G-C-F-E-G-C-E, was determined to be too complicated for the announcers to play correctly on a consistent basis. It was first simplified to G-C-F-E, and finally to just G-E-C. This familiar sequence was heard for the first time on November 29, 1929.
 The chimes were sounded at :29:30 and :59:30 of each hour, to indicate the start of the 30 second local station break. They were initially struck by hand by the announcer, using a set of hand-held chimes held up to the microphone.  But, there were inconsistencies in the chimes' tempo, volume, and exact timing.    It was finally determined that the best way to solve these problems was for the chimes to be generated mechanically.
     The man who designed the chimes machine was Captain Richard H. Ranger, who was also the inventor of the electronic organ and the RCA facsimile. Ranger created a device resembling a music box, where fingers on a revolving drum plucked a set of reeds. There were three sets of eight reeds, one for each note, allowing the generation of the fundamental note plus several overtones. Each reed formed one plate of a capacitor in an oscillator circuit, and the signal generated by all reeds was amplified by a single 6C6 pentode tube. It was activated by a timer,which would cut off the program two seconds before its end (whether it was finished or not!) and feed the chimes to the network.  NBC built a limited number of chime machines. NBC in San Francisco had two of them - the main and backup machines. Others were installed ain other cities around the country where network programs were originated – Los Angeles, Chicago, New York, and perhaps a few others. It is likely that not more than a dozen chimes machines were ever made.
      The photos on this page show one of the few chime machines still in existence, now in the hands of a private collector. (NBC had the short-sighted habit of discarding large quantities of historical artifacts throughout its history. It's only through the far-sightedness of a few NBC employees, who saved some of these items from the trash bins, that we can today experience many recorded programs, photos, and other memorabilia from that era.)  The unit shown is the chimes machine serial number 2, probably from the first group ever made. Its mechanical parts, although finely crafted, appear to have been hand made. This unit is no doubt the original chimes machine placed in operation at NBC's studios at 111 Sutter Street in San Francisco. The schematic diagram, also shown, indicates that serial number 5 was fabricated in 1933, so this machine would have predated it. The main cabinet contains the motor drive reed mechanism and amplifier, which is accessed by removing the front panel's four thumbscrews. The unit operated from an external power source, no doubt the same battery and motor generator system that operated the audio amplifiers in the studios. The smaller box contains the timer and switches that operate the chimes for both studio and "NEMO" broadcast lines. ("NEMO" was a term used in early radio to indicate a remote broadcast. It comes from a telephone term, and stands for "Not Emanating Main Office".) The chime machine could be operated in an automatic mode by the clock, which was the usual method of operation, or manually by the announcer in the event of programs with imprecise ending times, such as sports broadcasts.
       The NBC chimes were officially registered with the U.S. Patent Office in 1950 as a registered service mark, the first known case of a sound receiving trademark protection. They were last heard regularly on NBC television in 1976, used to mark the 50th anniversary of the network.
   Here is a remembrance of the NBC Chimes Machine from Rick Greenhut - February 3, 2013:
  Just saw your page on the Bay Area Radio Museum dedicated to the NBC chimes machine, and I wanted to give you another historical fact to add. In 1969 when I went to work as a summer replacement engineer (board op) at NBC-owned WKYC Cleveland, there was still a chimes machine back in the racks. Since all the O&O's originated programs like NOTH (New On The Hour) and Monitor inserts, they all had 2 chimes machines. Besides WNBC/New York, WMAQ/Chicago and KNBR/San Francisco, WKYC/Cleveland and WRC/Washington also had chimes machines.
  By the next summer I worked there, the chimes machine was gone - whether thrown away or taken home by one of the old-timers, I couldn't say.
  Last bit of radio trivia: I was doing Affiliate Relations for the NBC Radio Network in August of 1987, and during the NABET engineers strike those of us in management with technical backgrounds were taking shifts running the board for the NOTH and manning the ROD (Radio Operations Desk) in Radio Central (network master control). I was the first management person on the board when the engineers walked out at 6:00 AM that morning, and engineered the 6, 7, 8 and 9 AM NOTH (the talent was Gary Nunn, now heard on the CBS Radio Network). At 8:30 that morning I was told by the General Manager, Craig Simon, that the network had been sold to Westwood One, and that the chimes were not part of the sale, so I was forbidden to play them at the end of the newscast like we usually did.
  I ignored him, and "chimed out" at the end of the 9:00 AM newscast as per usual. He came up to the studio a few minutes later with a funny smile on his face, and told me to make sure no one else did that. I made sure by taking the chimes cart and backup out of the studio. I bulked the backup, and have the last remaining cart with the NBC chimes (recorded directly from the WNBC chimes machine) in my collection.
      REFERENCES: A History of the NBC Chimes, by Bill Harris More on the NBC Chimes, by Brian Wickham A Backstage Visit to Radio City, by Fred Krock Author's inspection of a chimes machine in the hands of a private collector

note:
投资者的远程投资困境从一开始就很明显：向你从未谋面的人投资数百万美元是一件具有挑战性的事情，而且从提前几天就安排好的“即兴”对话中学到的东西不多。一些投资者表示，大流行迫使他们在有经验的类别中坚持与他们认识的人在一起，限制了一个人可以将资金推向的网络。
然而，在全球大流行六个多月后，新技术正在涌现，以解决其中一些灾难。从尽职调查到采购，交易的艺术正在从文化和技术的角度发生变化。
Matchbox.VC(前身为Fortnite.VC)是重建非正式联系和同志情谊的新地方之一。
这项服务通过视频游戏将创始人和投资者与网络联系起来，并在压力较小的环境中寻找交易来源。Matchbox.VC的灵感来自于创始人基金(Founders Fund)负责人德利安·阿斯帕鲁霍夫(Delian Asparouhov)的一条推文，并吸引了Tribe Capital的阿尔琼·塞西(Arjun Sethi)、Blockstack的前创始人瑞安·谢伊(Ryan Shea)、AlphafundVC的杰克·查普曼(Jack Chapman)和Betaworks的彼得·罗哈斯(Peter Rojas)等投资者的兴趣。它的最后一场比赛之夜得到了YAC、部落资本和耸肩资本的支持。
我们在这家公司总共投资了1400万美元，这是一场竞赛，在一个令人满意的世界里是反周期的，所以是的。
推介很简单：创始人和投资者在网站上注册，回答关于他们的关注点、公司和阶段的基本问题，然后从包括堡垒之夜、cod：warzone和Valorant在内的8个选项中选择3个游戏。
note_en:
Remote investment struggles for investors were clear from the get go: it’s challenging to invest millions in someone you have never met, and there’s not a lot to learn from “off-the-cuff” conversations that are calendared days in advance. Some investors said the pandemic was  forcing them to stick with people they know in categories where they have experience, limiting the network that one can push money into.
 Over six months into a global pandemic, though, new techniques are emerging to address some of these woes. The very art of a deal, from due diligence to sourcing, is changing from a cultural and technological standpoint.
 One of the new places that recreates informal bonding and camaraderie is  Matchbox.VC, formerly Fortnite.VC.
 The service connects founders and investors over video games to network and source deals in a low-stress environment. Matchbox.VC was inspired from a tweet by Founders Fund principal Delian Asparouhov and has garnered interest from investors like Arjun Sethi from Tribe Capital, Ryan Shea, the ex-founder of Blockstack, Jake Chapman from AlphafundVC and Peter Rojas from Betaworks. Its last game night was backed by Yac, Tribe Capital and Shrug Capital.
 We’ve invested $14m total in the company and it’s off to the races, and is counter cyclical in a covid world so yeah
 The pitch is simple: founders and investors sign up on the website, answer basic questions about their focus, company and stage before picking three game choices from eight options that include Fortnite, COD: Warzone and Valorant.
title: 随着新冠肺炎时代的拖延，风投们将目光投向了Zoom呼吁的尽职调查和采购之外
title_en: As COVID-19 era drags on, VCs look beyond Zoom calls for due diligence and sourcing

(彭博社)--VMware Inc.。接受公司提议成为长期远程员工的员工，如果从美国生活成本最高的硅谷搬到一个生活成本较低的城市，他们的工资将会降低。
这家软件制造商已经加入了Facebook Inc.等科技公司的行列。和Twitter Inc.。在冠状病毒大流行之后，它允许一些办公室工作人员选择在家中永久工作。但知情人士称，例如，在VMware加州帕洛阿尔托总部工作并前往丹佛的员工必须接受18%的降薪。这些知情人士说，离开硅谷去洛杉矶或圣地亚哥意味着放弃8%的年薪。由于讨论内部政策，这些知情人士要求不具名。
Facebook和Twitter等其他科技公司已经实施或正在考虑类似的薪酬政策。Twitter的一位发言人表示，该公司在薪酬本地化方面采取了一种“竞争性”的方式，而Facebook则公开表示，可能会根据员工选择的搬迁地点来削减员工的工资。
VMware负责人力资源的高级副总裁里奇·朗(Rich Lang)表示，公司根据不同地区的“劳动力成本”和竞争员工的公司之间的基准工资差异来调整工资。虽然一些员工会看到减薪，但朗表示，如果其他员工选择搬到更大或更昂贵的城市，他们可能会加薪。
冠状病毒大流行已经改变了美国人的生活，迫使数百万人在家工作。一些美国人搬到离家人和亲人更近的地方，或者搬到比沿海昂贵的大都市中心更负担得起的地区。在高失业率的经济衰退中，许多技术工作者在决定大流行后他们希望在哪里工作以及如何工作方面比以往任何时候都有更大的灵活性。但一些公司在员工离开硅谷(美国生活成本最高的地方之一)时追回部分补偿的努力表明，离开可能是有代价的。
云软件制造商ServiceNow Inc.。据首席执行官比尔·麦克德莫特(Bill McDermott)称，该公司正在考虑从2021年开始改变湾区搬走工人的工资。
麦克德莫特周五在接受采访时表示：“我不认为我们应该有一个管理层不参与这些决定的环境，因为你可能会进入这样一种情况，即员工在任何地方都会成为工作的决策者，如果是这样的话，你将很难组织和保持一种文化。”
麦克德莫特说，最终决定尚未做出，这家总部位于加利福尼亚州圣克拉拉的公司将试图制定一项官方政策。他说，ServiceNow未来可能会有一支混合型员工队伍，员工在办公室、路上和家里。
“当他们在科罗拉多州的山区呆了几个月，在那里他们感到更安全的时候，我们会调整他们的工资吗？可能不会。“他补充说。
根据美国经济分析局(U.S.Bureau of Economic Analysis)的数据，截至2018年，旧金山湾区的商品和服务价格(包括租金)是美国大都市地区中最高的。纽约市的城市中心位居第二。在各种规模的地区中，位于硅谷中心的加州城市圣何塞、桑尼维尔和圣克拉拉的租金是美国最高的。
由于旧金山湾区的企业园区因当地的冠状病毒规定而仍处于关闭状态，长期以来习惯于善待上班族以促进留住的科技公司将远程工作作为另一项福利，但员工将不得不决定离开北加州是否值得失去的工资。出于工资和税收的原因，这些公司必须知道员工的居住地，但是否浮动薪酬是他们的自由裁量权。VMware的朗说，公司试图向员工透明地说明搬家会如何影响他们的薪酬，这样他们就可以在知情的情况下做出决定。
“我们要求员工坦率和诚实，因为这是VMware的期望，但政府也要求你根据工作地点缴税，”朗说。他承认，即使搬到其他地方，也会有一些员工继续申请加州的地址。如果工人临时搬家与家人在一起或其他短期原因，他们的工资不会受到影响。
除了薪酬政策，Twitter还为所有员工提供了1000美元的一次性在家工作津贴，无论他们身在何处，并增加了休息天数。VMware还给了所有员工两周的自由假期，这样他们就可以充电了。
脸书在5月份告诉员工，公司将很快更永久地过渡到远程工作，即使在新冠肺炎辞职后也是如此。然而，离开旧金山或纽约等生活成本较高地区的员工将不得不减薪，这取决于他们截至2021年1月1日的居住地。首席执行官马克·扎克伯格(Mark Zuckerberg)说，他预计在未来5到10年内，Facebook全球员工中将有多达50%的人在异地工作。
拥有VMware 81%股份的个人电脑制造商戴尔技术公司(Dell Technologies Inc.)已经要求志愿者在疫情爆发后每周一到五天在家工作，只要他们获得了经理的批准。这家总部位于得克萨斯州圆石城的公司在一份声明中表示，“目前”它不会对薪酬做出任何调整，并将为远程员工提供400美元的家庭办公设备一次性津贴。
note_en:
(Bloomberg) -- VMware Inc. employees who take up the company’s offer to become permanent remote workers will get a pay cut if they move from Silicon Valley, one of the nation’s most costly areas to live, to a less-expensive city.
 The software maker has joined technology companies such as Facebook Inc. and Twitter Inc. in letting some of its office staff choose to permanently work from home in the wake of the coronavirus pandemic. But employees who worked at VMware’s Palo Alto, California, headquarters and go to Denver, for example, must accept an 18% salary reduction, people familiar with the matter said. Leaving Silicon Valley for Los Angeles or San Diego means relinquishing 8% of their annual pay, said the people, who asked not to be identified discussing internal policies.
 Facebook and Twitter are among the other technology companies that have put in place or are considering similar pay policies. A Twitter spokesman said the company has a “competitive” approach to localizing compensation while Facebook has publicly said it may cut employees’ salaries depending on where they choose to move.
  VMware’s senior vice president of human resources, Rich Lang, said the company adjusts salary based on the “cost of labor” in different regional zones and benchmarks salary variations among firms competing for its workers. While some employees will see pay cuts, Lang said others could get a raise if they chose to move to a larger or more expensive city.
 The coronavirus pandemic has shuffled U.S. life, forcing millions of people to work from home. Some Americans have moved closer to their families and loved ones or to more affordable areas than the expensive metropolitan hubs along the coasts. In the midst of a recession with high unemployment, many technology workers have greater flexibility than ever before in deciding where and how they want to work post-pandemic. But some companies’ efforts to claw back some compensation when workers depart Silicon Valley, one of America’s most expensive places to live, shows that leaving may have its costs.
 Cloud-software maker ServiceNow Inc. is considering changing the pay of Bay Area workers who move away, starting in 2021, according to Chief Executive Officer Bill McDermott.
 “I don’t believe we should have an environment where management is not involved with those determinations, because what you could get into here is a situation where employees then become the decision-maker in working literally from anywhere, and you would have a hard time organizing and holding together a culture if that was the case,” McDermott said Friday in an interview.
 McDermott said a final decision hasn’t been made, and the Santa Clara, California-based company will try to create an official policy. He said ServiceNow probably will have a hybrid workforce in the future, with employees in offices, on the road and at home.
 “Are we going to adjust their pay when they’re in the mountains of Colorado for a couple of months where they feel safer? Probably not,” he added.
 The San Francisco Bay Area had the highest prices for goods and services, including rent, among large metropolitan areas in the country as of 2018, according to data from the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis. The New York City urban hub was in second place. Among areas of all sizes, the California cities of San Jose, Sunnyvale and Santa Clara, at the heart of Silicon Valley, had the highest rents in the U.S.
 As corporate campuses in the Bay Area remain closed because of local coronavirus rules, tech companies long used to treating their office workers well to boost retention have pitched remote work as yet another perk, but employees will have to decide if leaving Northern California is worth the lost wages. For payroll and tax reasons, these companies must know where employees reside, but the decision to fluctuate pay is at their discretion. VMware’s Lang said the company has tried to be transparent with employees about how moving would affect their compensation, so they can make an informed decision.
 “We are asking employees to be upfront and honest, because that’s the expectation at VMware, but also the governments require you pay your taxes based on where you work,” Lang said, acknowledging there will be some workers who continue to claim a California address even if they move elsewhere. If workers temporarily move to be with family or another short-term reason, their pay will not be affected.
  Separate from the pay policies, Twitter has provided all employees a one-time $1,000 work-from-home allowance regardless of their locations and additional days of rest. VMware has also given all employees two weeks off at their discretion so they can recharge.
 Facebook told employees in May that the company would soon transition more permanently to remote work, even after Covid-19 subsides. Employees who leave expensive areas like San Francisco or New York will have to take a pay cut, however, depending on where they live as of Jan. 1, 2021. Chief Executive Officer Mark Zuckerberg said he expects as much 50% of Facebook’s global workforce to be remote in the next five to 10 years.
 Dell Technologies Inc., the personal computer maker that owns 81% of VMware, has asked for volunteers to work from home as much as they would like, from one to five days a week post-pandemic, as long as they have manager approval. The Round Rock, Texas-based company said in a statement that it isn’t making any adjustments to compensation “at this point in time,” and will give remote workers a one-time stipend of $400 for home-office equipment.
title: VMware削减逃离硅谷的远程员工的工资
title_en: VMware Cuts Pay for Remote Workers Fleeing Silicon Valley
